quiz.title: Geometry: Bearings Applications
quiz.subtitle: Unit 8 Quiz 11: Navigation Problems Using the Law of Sines and Law of Cosines (10 Questions)
quiz.subject: Mathematics

questions.question: A true bearing is measured as:
options: The angle clockwise from North, ranging from 000° to 360°
The angle counterclockwise from East, ranging from 0° to 90°
The angle clockwise from South, ranging from 000° to 180°
The angle between two moving ships
questions.explanation: A true bearing is always measured clockwise from North, from 000° to 360°. For example, East is 090°, South is 180°, West is 270°. This is different from compass quadrant bearings (like N 30° E).

questions.question: Ship A sails from port on a bearing of 040°. Ship B sails from the same port on a bearing of 115°. What is the angle between their paths?
questions.explanation: The angle between the two bearings is the difference: 115° - 40° = 75°. This angle is the included angle at the port, which can be used in the Law of Cosines to find the distance between the ships.

questions.question: A ship sails from port on a bearing of 045° for 20 miles, then changes course to a bearing of 120° for 25 miles. What is the distance from the starting point to the ship's final position, rounded to the nearest tenth of a mile?
questions.explanation: The angle between the two legs is 120° - 45° = 75°. Use Law of Cosines (SAS): d² = 20² + 25² - 2(20)(25)·cos(75°). d² = 400 + 625 - 1000·cos(75°). With cos(75°) ≈ 0.2588: d² = 1025 - 258.8 = 766.2. d = √766.2 ≈ 27.7 miles.

questions.question: A plane flies from an airport on a bearing of 155° for 300 miles, then changes course to a bearing of 230° for 250 miles. What is the distance from the airport to the plane's final position, rounded to the nearest tenth of a mile?
Two ships leave the same port at the same time. One sails on a bearing of 030° for 18 miles. The other sails on a bearing of 120° for 24 miles. How far apart are the ships?
questions.explanation: The angle between their paths is 120° - 30° = 90°. With a 90° angle, cos(90°) = 0, so the Law of Cosines reduces to the Pythagorean Theorem: d² = 18² + 24² = 324 + 576 = 900. d = 30 miles. This is a scaled 3-4-5 right triangle.

questions.question: From port P, lighthouse A is on a bearing of 020° at a distance of 15 miles. Lighthouse B is on a bearing of 080° at an unknown distance. From lighthouse A, the bearing to lighthouse B is 130°. Find the distance from port P to lighthouse B, rounded to the nearest tenth of a mile.
questions.explanation: In △PAB: ∠APB = 80° - 20° = 60°. At A: bearing back to P = 200°, bearing to B = 130°. Interior ∠PAB = 200° - 130° = 70°. Then ∠PBA = 180° - 60° - 70° = 50°. Using Law of Sines: sin(70°)/PB = sin(50°)/15. PB = 15·sin(70°)/sin(50°) ≈ 15(0.9397)/(0.7660) ≈ 18.4 miles.

questions.question: In △ABC, ∠A = 45°, ∠B = 70°, and side c (opposite ∠C) = 25. Find side a (opposite ∠A), rounded to the nearest tenth.
questions.explanation: First find ∠C = 180° - 45° - 70° = 65°. Using Law of Sines: sin(45°)/a = sin(65°)/25. Cross-multiplying: a = 25·sin(45°)/sin(65°). With sin(45°) ≈ 0.7071 and sin(65°) ≈ 0.9063: a ≈ 25(0.7071)/(0.9063) ≈ 19.5. The Law of Sines is ideal here because two angles and a side are known (AAS).

questions.question: A ship sails from port 50 miles on a bearing of 060° to point A, then 40 miles on a bearing of 150° to point B. What is the bearing from the port to point B, rounded to the nearest degree?
questions.explanation: At A, the direction back to port is 060° + 180° = 240°. The direction to B is 150°. Interior angle at A = 240° - 150° = 90°. PB² = 50² + 40² - 2(50)(40)·cos(90°) = 2500 + 1600 = 4100. PB = √4100 ≈ 64.0 miles. Using Law of Sines for angle at P: sin(∠APB)/40 = sin(90°)/64.0. sin(∠APB) = 40/64.0 ≈ 0.625. ∠APB ≈ 38.7°. Bearing = 060° + 38.7° ≈ 099°.

questions.question: A ship sails from port 20 miles on a bearing of 030° to point A, then 15 miles on a bearing of 110° to point B. What is the bearing from point B back to port, rounded to the nearest degree?

Ship A leaves port at noon on a bearing of 040° at 18 knots. Ship B leaves the same port at noon on a bearing of 100° at 24 knots. How far apart are the ships after 2 hours, rounded to the nearest tenth of a nautical mile?
questions.explanation: After 2 hours: Ship A travels 18 × 2 = 36 nautical miles on 040°. Ship B travels 24 × 2 = 48 nautical miles on 100°. Angle between paths = 100° - 40° = 60°. d² = 36² + 48² - 2(36)(48)·cos(60°). cos(60°) = 0.5. d² = 1296 + 2304 - 3456(0.5) = 3600 - 1728 = 1872. d = √1872 = √(144 × 13) = 12√13 ≈ 43.3 nautical miles.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered navigation problems using bearings with the Law of Sines and Law of Cosines. You can interpret true bearings, find angles between paths, and solve for distances and return bearings.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Remember: (1) the angle between two bearings is their difference, (2) use Law of Cosines when you have SAS or SSS, (3) use Law of Sines when you have AAS or ASA, (4) a bearing back to the start is the forward bearing plus 180° (adjust if over 360°).
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Focus on: (1) drawing a clear diagram with North reference lines at each point, (2) finding interior angles from bearing differences, (3) choosing Law of Sines vs. Law of Cosines based on given information, (4) converting an angle back to a true bearing.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! True bearings are measured clockwise from North. Draw the triangle formed by the paths, find the interior angles, then apply the Law of Sines (sin A/a = sin B/b = sin C/c) or Law of Cosines (c² = a² + b² - 2ab·cos C). The bearing to return is 180° more than the bearing out.
